title: Understanding the Problem
text: The jumper's distance depends on the angle of takeoff. We need to calculate the distance at $$30$$ degrees and compare it to the distance at $$45$$ degrees.

title: Projectile Motion Formula
text: The range of a projectile is given by the formula: \( R $$=$$ \frac{v**2 \sin(2\theta)}{g} \), where \( v \) is the initial velocity, \( $$\theta$$ \) is the angle of takeoff, and \( g \) is the acceleration due to gravity.

title: Calculate Range at $$30$$ Degrees
text: Use the formula \( R $$=$$ \frac{v**2 \sin(2\theta)}{g} \) to calculate the range at $$30$$ degrees. Remember, \( \sin(60) $$=$$ \sqrt{3}/2 \).

title: Calculate Range at $$45$$ Degrees
text: Calculate the range at $$45$$ degrees using the formula \( R $$=$$ \frac{v**2 \sin(2\theta)}{g} \).

title: Calculate Range at $$30$$ Degrees
text: Calculate the range at $$30$$ degrees using the formula \( R $$=$$ \frac{v**2 \sin(2\theta)}{g} \).

title: Find the Difference
text: Subtract the range at $$30$$ degrees from the range at $$45$$ degrees to find the distance lost.

title: Calculate Distance Lost
text: Subtract the range at $$30$$ degrees from the range at $$45$$ degrees: \( $$8.0$$ - $$6.93$$ \).
